coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric sensor based on dual-emission carbon dots sensitive detection of amaranth
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Lin
Liu, Lizhen
Mi, Zhi
Chen, Meng
Bai, Yunfeng
Qin, Jun
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Amaranth (AMA), a common food additive, is important to strictly control the content of food for the human body. In this paper, an innovative method based on intrinsic dual-emissive carbon dots (Y/B-CDs) was used to detect AMA. Y/B-CDs have two emission wavelengths at 416 and 544 nm with the excitation wavelength at 362 nm. The addition of AMA can rapidly quench the fluorescence of the two peaks with different degrees, and ratiometric detection can be achieved. Quantitative analysis showed two linear ranges of 0.1–20 μM and 20–80 μM, and detection limits are 42 and 33 nM, respectively. Moreover, good results were obtained for the detection of AMA in beverages and candy using Y/B-CDs. This suggests that the constructed sensor has the potential to detect AMA in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission carbon dots
Amaranth
Ratiometric sensor
Beverages

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric detection of AMA real samples
Determination of AMA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)XPS Full spectrum, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s, (E) S 2p spectra, (F) FTIR spectra of the Y/B-CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the Y/B-CDs, (B) Emission spectra of Y/B-CDs at 350–400 nm excitation wavelengths.
(A) The fluorescence spectrum of Y/B-CDs as AMA increases from 0.1 to 80 µM, (B) and (C) are the linear equations.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of AMA and the fluorescence spectra of Y/B-CDs, (B) fluorescence lifetime of Y/B-CDs and Y/B-CDs + AMA.

(A) Correction (red curve) and observation (black curve) suppressed efficiency (E%) of Y/B-CDs after adding different concentrations of AMA, E = 1 − F/F0. (B) Stern-Volmer plot of AMA quenched Y/B-CDs fluorescence after the IFE correction.
(A) F416/F544 of Y/B-CDs in the presence of interfering substances, (B) F416/F544 of Y/B-CDs when AMA and interfering substances coexist.
the fabrication of Y/B-CDs and the detection of AMA.
Analytical results of AMA in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A ratiometric sensor based on dual-emission carbon dots sensitive detection of amaranth

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School Department of Energy Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Shanxi Institute of Energy, Taiyuan 030600, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School Department of Energy Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School Department of Energy Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Shanxi Institute of Energy, Taiyuan 030600, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China (F. Feng).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of Y/B-CDs and the detection of AMA.

Amaranth (AMA), a common food additive, is important to strictly control the content of food for the human body. In this paper, an innovative method based on intrinsic dual-emissive carbon dots (Y/B-CDs) was used to detect AMA. Y/B-CDs have two emission wavelengths at 416 and 544 nm with the excitation wavelength at 362 nm. The addition of AMA can rapidly quench the fluorescence of the two peaks with different degrees, and ratiometric detection can be achieved. Quantitative analysis showed two linear ranges of 0.1–20 μM and 20–80 μM, and detection limits are 42 and 33 nM, respectively. Moreover, good results were obtained for the detection of AMA in beverages and candy using Y/B-CDs. This suggests that the constructed sensor has the potential to detect AMA in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, azo dyes have played a pivotal role in the food industry because of their good coloring power [1]. Among them is amaranth (Fig.S1, AMA), whose low cost and high stability make it popular for adding an eye-catching red color to beverages, syrups, and candies [2,3]. Although it can beautify the appearance of food and increase its attractiveness, excessive consumption can also cause serious health hazards, for example, cytotoxicity, and high genotoxicity [4,5]. It also release aromatic amines into the organism leading to liver damage, kidney, and potential cancer risk [6]. Therefore, it is of great significance to measure the content of AMA in food. Several methods have been reported, such as, electrochemistry [7], spectrophotometric [8], thin-layer chromatography [9], and capillary electrophoresis [10]. Tajik [11] used a novel Ni–Mo-MOF-modified screen printed electrode to detect amaranth, and the limit of detection as low as 0.05 ± 0.001 µM under optimal conditions. Wang [12] used Co3O4-CeO2/graphene modified electrodes as amaranth electrochemical sensors. Cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry experiments show that the sensor can sensitively detect amaranth with good linearity in the concentration range of 2–96 µM. These methods can detect AMA sensitively, but time-consuming sample pretreatment, expensive instrument, and complex operations limit their application. The fluorescence method, which is a better trace analysis method than these, has the merits of easy operation, rapid response, sensitivity, and low cost [13,14].

Carbon dots is a kind of material with a diameter of less than 10 nm, which has attracted the attention of researchers in various fields since its discovery in 2004 [15]. Carbon dots have the merits of good water solubility, excellent photostability, and strong fluorescence [16,17]. These excellent properties make carbon dots become a research hotspot in the field of fluorescent sensing [18]. For example, Liu et al. [19] constructed a way to detect AMA through carbon dots. According to the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots, the quantitative analysis of AMA can be realized in the range of 0.2–30 µM. Xiang et al. [20] synthesized N,S-CDs from methionine and o-phenylenediamine can achieve the detection of AMA content in beverages and strawberry jam through the static quenching process (SQE) and the inner filter effect (IFE). Although these sensors perform well in detection, the shortcoming that the response signal is easily influenced by the surrounding environment also limits their application [21,22]. The ratiometric sensor with dual emission wavelengths at a single excitation wavelength can solve this problem perfectly. The intrinsic self-calibration function of the ratiometric sensor can greatly enhance the accuracy of detection and avoid the effects of the external environment, light source fluctuations, and other factors. These advantages make it more competitive in actual sample testing [23,24].

In this work, we constructed yellow/blue dual-emission carbon dots (Y/B-CDs) using the raw materials sulfanilic acid and resorcinol. The Y/B-CDs show strong fluorescence emission and excellent water solubility. When the excitation wavelength is 362 nm, Y/B-CDs display attractive 416 and 544 nm dual-emission wavelengths. The addition of AMA will cause the fluorescence of the two emission peaks to be quenched in different degrees, the emission peak at 544 nm has a greater impact and the emission peak at 416 nm is less. Therefore, AMA can be detected quantitatively depending on the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio (F416/F544). It is worth mentioning that this is the first time detect AMA using a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual-emission Y/B-CDs. In addition, the sensor can also realize the analysis of AMA in real samples. Scheme 1 shows the fabrication of Y/B-CDs and the detection of AMA.

AMA and brilliant blue were bought from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, AlCl3, sulfanilic acid, resorcinol, sucrose, vitamin C, vitamin B1, lactose, glucose, and maltose were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China) provided citric acid, sodium citrate, sodium benzoate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4).

Used Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo Fischer, USA) for transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis. F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to acquire fluorescence spectrums of y/r-CDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was completed using Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA). QM8000 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan) was used to collect data for the fluorescence lifetime. The UV–vis spectrogram was acquired using a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was evaluated through K-Alpha (Thermo Fischer, USA). Raman Spectroscopy was completed using invia Qontor (Renishaw, UK).

The Y/B-CDs were produced via the hydrothermal method using resorcinol and sulfanilic acid. Add 0.2 g sulfanilic acid in 20 mL ultrapure water to dissolve by heating, and then add 0.2 g resorcinol and stir to form a clarified solution. After that, transfer the transparent solution to a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and react at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction is finished and the solution has cooled, it is filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 µM). Then, the solution was purified for 24 h using a dialysis tube (MWCO, 500 Da) to remove unreacted particles. At last, freeze-drying was used to gather the Y/B-CDs powder for subsequent use.

For AMA determination, 70 μL of the Y/B-CDs solution (20 mg·mL−1) was mixed with 2930 μL of PBS solution (0.01 M, pH = 6), then various volumes of AMA solution were added and mixed well. After incubation for 1 min, the emission spectra of Y/B-CDs at 362 nm excitation wavelength were recorded. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were measured at 700 V with both excitation and emission slits of 5 nm. Then, the selectivity of the Y/B-CDs system for AMA was researched. Add Y/B-CDs into the solution with and without interfering substances and record the F416/F544 of Y/B-CDs, interfering substance including citric acid, sodium citrate, vitamin C, sucrose, sodium benzoate, acesulfame, brilliant blue, vitamin B1, glucose, lactose, maltose, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, and AlCl3. In addition, the concentrations of interfering substances and AMA in the interference experiment were the same as 10 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of AMA real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To study the feasibility of the sensor, carbonated drinks, soda water, and candy were selected as the detection objects. Drinks and candy are purchased from local supermarkets. Both drinks are filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 µM). Carbonated drinks contained lots of carbon dioxide that needed to be ultrasonically processed for 30 min before use. Diluting 1 mL of the drink with PBS (pH = 6) to 50 mL for later use. Dissolve 2 g candy sample in 10 mL ultrapure water and boil for 10 min to remove carbon dioxide before diluting to 100 mL with PBS (pH = 6) for further testing. Different volumes of the AMA solution were added into the samples to produce spiked samples. In the experiment, record the fluorescence spectra of 2.93 mL sample solution and 70 μL Y/B-CDs solution. The content of AMA in the sample was calculated by linear equations.

The morphology and particle size of the Y/B-CDs were analyzed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the Y/B-CDs are uniformly dispersed and appear spherical. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 1A) did not observe lattice fringes indicating the amorphous structure of Y/B-CDs. According to Raman spectroscopy (Figure S2), graphite and disordered carbon appear in the D band at 1333 cm−1 and the G band at 1542 cm−1, respectively [25]. The strength of D over G band suggests that numerous structural defects exist in the Y/B-CDs that lead to the poor crystallinity of the graphite [26]. By randomly counting about 100 Y/B-CDs particles on the TEM image, the particle size distribution of Y/B-CDs was between 2.73 and 5.08 nm with an average particle size of 3.95 nm (Fig. 1B).

Use XPS to analyze the element composition of Y/B-CDs in detail. The five peaks at 168.84, 231.83, 285, 401.32, and 531.9 eV are displayed on the full spectrum (Fig. 2 A), corresponding to S 2p, S 2s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. Then the high-resolution spectra of four elements are processed by peak fitting. The C 1s spectra (Fig. 2B) appeared three bands at 283.93 eV (C–C/CC), 284.67 eV (C-N/C-O/C-S), and 285.92 eV (CN), respectively [27]. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2C) revealed three peaks at 400.71, 401.52, and 402.41 eV are ascribed to CC–C, C-N-C, and N–H, respectively [28]. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 2D) demonstrated peaks corresponding to CO/SO at 531.2 eV, C-O at 531.93 eV, and O–H group at 533.12 eV [29]. The S 2p spectra (Fig. 2E) contained three peaks at 167.51, 168.44, and 169.59 eV, which belong to C-S/N-S, SO, and –C-SOx (x = 2,3,4), respectively [30]. The S and N elements in the Y/B-CDs are derived from sulfanilic acid. Next, FTIR was utilized to study the surface functional groups of Y/B-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2F, the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH2 groups appeared at 3204.08 and 3055.1 cm−1 [31]. The stretching vibration of C–H occurred at 2891.84 cm−1 [32]. Absorption bands at 1604.08, 1489.8 and 1377.55 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of CC, CN, and C-N groups, respectively [33,34]. Other peaks at 1216.33, 1151.02, and 1132.65 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of C-S, C-O, and –SO3 -, respectively [35,36]. XPS and FTIR data show that the surface of the Y/B-CDs contains a considerable amount of –NH2 and –OH groups, which guarantees the outstanding water solubility of the Y/B-CDs.

The optical characteristics of Y/B-CDs were examined in three aspects: UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra, and absolute fluorescence quantum yields (QY). Fig. 3 A shows that UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) have two apparent peaks located at 246 and 274 nm, which originate from the π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CN/SO. The optimal excitation wavelength (red line) of the Y/B-CDs appears at 362 nm, and its corresponding emission spectrum (blue line) occurs at 416 and 544 nm, respectively. In addition, the Y/B-CDs solution is yellow in natural light and emits yellow fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). Fig. 3B displays the emission spectra of Y/B-CDs at various excitation wavelengths (350–400 nm). As the excitation wavelength changes, the emission wavelength also changes. This is due to the different types of surface functional groups on the surface of CDs causing a series of energy levels between π-π*, resulting in many different emission sites. At a specific excitation wavelength, a specific emission site will dominate, and as the excitation wavelength changes, the corresponding emission site will dominate [37,38]. Furthermore, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of Y/B-CDs is 0.75%. Next, the stability of Y/B-CDs is studied. The F416/F544 of Y/B-CDs has no significant change either in 0–1 mM salt solution or at Xe lamp irradiation for 1 h, and the change of F416/F544 was small after one month (Fig.S3). These phenomena indicate that the Y/B-CDs have favorable stability.

In order to make the sensor more sensitive, the parameters of pH and incubation time were optimized. Because the fluorescence peak at 544 nm of Y/B-CDs would quench immediately in the alkaline solution, the acidic environment was chosen to optimize the conditions. The AMA used in the experiments was all 10 µM, and the change of F416/F544 before and after the addition of AMA (ΔF) in PBS with different pH was recorded. As Fig. S4 A shows, when the pH is increased from 2 to 6, the ΔF gradually increases and reaches its biggest value at PH = 6. Therefore, PBS with pH = 6 was selected for the following experiment. Fig. S4B depicts the change of the F416/F544 within 1 h after adding AMA, and the F416/F544 reached the maximum value in 1 min and remained stable within 1 h. In consequence, pH = 6 and 1 min incubation time were the ideal conditions for sensing AMA.

In the experiment, we structured a ratiometric probe, whose self-calibration function can avoid the influence of factors such as external environment and light source fluctuations thus greatly improving the accuracy of detection. For AMA detection, as shown in Fig. 4A, when AMA was added into the solution of Y/B-CDs, two fluorescence emission peaks of the Y/B-CDs undergo quenching with different degrees, of which the peak at 544 nm decreases more and the peak at 416 nm decreases moderately. From the relationship between F416/F544 and the concentration of AMA in the range of 0.1–20 µM and 20–80 µM two linear equations can be obtained, which are y = 0.0649[C] + 1.2697 (R2 = 0.998) and y = 0.0836[C] + 0.9529 (R2 = 0.9988), respectively, where C denotes the concentration of AMA. The limits of detection (LODs) were calculated by the formulae LOD = 3 s/k (k is the slope of the calibration curve, s is the standard deviation of the blank solution (n = 11) as 42 and 33 nM, respectively. Compared with other reported methods, our sensor has lower LODs and wider linear ranges (Table S1).

First of all, the relationship between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AMA and the fluorescence spectra of Y/B-CDs was studied. As shown in Fig. 5 A, UV–vis absorption spectrum of AMA overlap partially with both the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Y/B-CDs (yellow area), which is consistent with the conditions for the occurrence of the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism and the IFE [39]. There is a big difference between FRET and IFE. If there is a FERT mechanism between AMA and Y/B-CDs will cause the fluorescence lifetime of Y/B-CDs to change while IFE will not [40]. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of Y/B-CDs and Y/B-CDs + AMA systems was analyzed next. The fluorescence lifetime of Y/B-CDs was 1.76 ns (Fig. 5B), and the lifetime of Y/B-CDs + AMA was 1.75 ns following Eq. (1) [41].
(1)
y =
A 1
e
- x /
t 1
+
A 2
e
- x /
t 2
where A1 and A2 are the related amplitudes, t1 and t2 are the time coefficients of the decay curves. So the FRET and the dynamic quenching effects (DQE) that can change the fluorescence lifetime were excluded [42]. Based on the above analysis, we speculate that there is IFE between AMA and Y/B-CDs.

Based on the absorption parameters of the AMA and Y/B-CDs mixtures and the geometry of the cuvette in fluorescence analysis, IFE was calculated using Eq. (2) [43].
(2)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
ex
1 -
10
- s
A
em
where Fcor and Fobsd are the fluorescence intensity corrected by eliminating IFE from Fobsd and the detected fluorescence intensity, respectively, Eobsd and Ecor are the measured and corrected suppressed efficiency, respectively, the absorptions at excitation wavelength (λex = 362 nm) and emission wavelength (λem = 544 nm) are expressed by Aex and Aem, respectively. According to Fig.S5, d is the cuvette width (1.0 cm), g is the distance between the edge of excitation beam and the cuvette (0.4 cm), and s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.1 cm).

The fluorescence intensity and absorbance of Y/B-CDs at varied AMA concentrations are summarized in Table S2. At each concentration of AMA, the correction factor (CF) of IFE was determined. Fig. 6 A displays the measured and corrected fluorescence suppression efficiency of the Y/B-CDs. We discovered that half of the suppressed effect of the AMA was caused by IFE, and the other part probably contributed to the quenching effect of AMA on Y/B-CDs after the removal of IFE.

Two probable reasons for fluorescence quenching of Y/B-CDs are the DQE and the SQE. Since the fluorescence lifetime of the Y/B-CDs did not change after addition of AMA, we speculate that the AMA induced fluorescence quenching was due to SQE, except for IFE. To confirm our speculation, Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (3) was applied to theoretically analyze the SQE and DQE [44].
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]

Where the steady-state fluorescence intensity before and after addition of AMA to the Y/B-CDs is indicated by F0 and F, respectively, [Q] is the AMA concentration, and KSV is the quenching constant. The study found a linear relationship between the ratio of corrected Y/B-CDs fluorescence intensity (Fcor,0/Fcor) and AMA concentration (Fcor,0/Fcor = 0.011CAMA + 1.0165, R2 = 0.9903, Fig. 6B), which suggests the Stern-Volmer equation can be implemented to analyze our research model with KSV = 0.011 μM−1.

Due to the difference in affinity between fluorophore and quenching agents, which can affect SQE and DQE, KSV was analyzed to obtain suitable quenching types. For SQE, KSV is considered a ground-state association constant. In DQE, the KSV is calculated by the following equation (eq (4) [45].
(4)
K
SV
=
k q
×
τ 0

Where τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of Y/B-CDs without AMA, and kq is the quenching rate constant. For Y/B-CDs, the results showed that kq is 6.25 × 1012 M−1s−1 (τ0 = 1.76 ns, KSV = 0.011 μM−1). Since kq is much larger than the value of the molecules in solution for the diffusion control process (1.0 × 1010 M−1s−1), the possibility of DQE as AMA-induced fluorescence quenching was excluded. Therefore, we speculate that the AMA induced fluorescence quenching is attributable to SQE, with the exception of IFE.

Next, the selectivity of Y/B-CDs to AMA was examined. As observed in Fig. 7 A, the addition of different interfering substances (citric acid, sodium citrate, vitamin C, sucrose, sodium benzoate, acesulfame, brilliant blue, vitamin B1, glucose, lactose, maltose, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, and AlCl3) in the Y/B-CDs solution have no effect on the F416/F544 of the Y/B-CDs and only the addition of AMA changes obviously. This would suggest that Y/B-CDs have good selectivity for AMA. Fig. 7B shows that the F416/F544 of Y/B-CDs can still change significantly after adding AMA in the presence of interfering substances, which indicates that the Y/B-CDs based ratiometric sensor can detect AMA sensitively and accurately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AMA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the designed sensor was analyzed through measured AMA in drinks and candy. Two beverages containing AMA and without AMA were selected as test samples. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery of AMA determined by this sensor was between 97.53 and 106.45% and the RSD ≤ 3.63 (n = 3), which indicates that the Y/B-CDs based ratiometric sensor can determine AMA in real samples with high sensitivity.

We constructed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on Y/B-CDs for the detection of AMA. When the excitation wavelength is at 362 nm, the Y/B-CDs have two emission peaks at 416 and 544 nm. AMA can quench the fluorescence intensity of the two peaks of the Y/B-CDs at different degrees, therefore, two good linear relationships were obtained based on the F416/F544 and the concentration of AMA with low detection limits. The sensor shows excellent selectivity and specificity for AMA and can detect AMA sensitively even in the presence of interfering substances. It is noteworthy that this is the first report of AMA detection using dual-emission Y/B-CDs. Excitingly, the application of this sensor for the detection of AMA in drinks and candy has also achieved good results. To sum up, our proposed sensor with the merits of easy synthesis, high sensitivity, and rapid response can be implemented for the detection of AMA in real samples.

Lin Yuan: Methodology, Visualization, Data curation. Lizhen Liu: Supervision. Zhi Mi: Investigation. Meng Chen: Investigation. Yunfeng Bai: Investigation. Jun Qin: Investigation. Feng Feng: Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge forfinancial support from Higher Education Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (2021L375 and 2021L387).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Morphology-tuned preparation of nanostructured resorcinol-formaldehyde carbonized polymers as highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for amaranth
J. Electroanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ji, Y. Zhang, S. Yu, S. Hu, K. Wu. Morphology-tuned preparation of nanostructured resorcinol-formaldehyde carbonized polymers as highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for amaranth. Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry 779 (2016), 169-175. doi:10.1016/j.jelechem.2016.03.011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe(3)O(4)@rGO doped molecularly imprinted polymer membrane based on magnetic field directed self-assembly for the determination of amaranth
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Han, X. Wang, Z. Yang, W. Zhu, X. Zhou, H. Jiang. Fe(3)O(4)@rGO doped molecularly imprinted polymer membrane based on magnetic field directed self-assembly for the determination of amaranth. Talanta 123 (2014), 101-108. doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2014.01.060

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glassy carbon electrode modified with N-doped reduced graphene oxide sheets as an effective electrochemical sensor for amaranth detection
Materials (Basel)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Moradpour, H. Beitollahi, F.G. Nejad, A. Di Bartolomeo. Glassy carbon electrode modified with N-doped reduced graphene oxide sheets as an effective electrochemical sensor for amaranth detection. Materials (Basel) 15 (2022), 3011. doi:10.3390/ma15093011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of amaranth in food based on the enhancement effect of carbon nanotube film
J. Agric. Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wang, X. Hu, Q. Cheng, X. Zhao, X. Fu, K. Wu. Electrochemical detection of amaranth in food based on the enhancement effect of carbon nanotube film. Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry 58 (2010), 12112-12116. doi:10.1021/jf103263p

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A green, simple, and rapid detection for amaranth in candy samples based on the fluorescence quenching of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum qots
Food Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, S. Luo, L. Sun, D. Kong, J. Sheng, K. Wang, C. Dong. A green, simple, and rapid detection for amaranth in candy samples based on the fluorescence quenching of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum qots. Food Analytical Methods 12 (2019), 1658-1665. doi:10.1007/s12161-019-01505-8

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive detection of amaranth realized with upconversion nanoparticles-based solid sensor
J. Electrochem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Cao, Q. Min, W. Bian, L. Yang, J. Lei, M. Zhang, H. Ma, J. Qiu, X. Xu, X. Yu. Highly sensitive detection of amaranth realized with upconversion nanoparticles-based solid sensor. Journal of The Electrochemical Society 167 (2020). doi:10.1149/1945-7111/abb283
